observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
(a) Determine the accountable officer responsible for preparing the
bounced check, the signatory thereof, and the approving officer of the
disbursement voucher, if different from the signatory, and demand
reimbursement of the bank charge as they are all jointly liable;
(b) Exert effort to substantiate the recorded receivables from funding
agencies and promptly enforce the municipality’s claim based on the
Memorandum of Agreement and/or applicable rules and regulations; and
(c) Refer to the Revised Chart of Accounts when assigning account names
during budget preparation to ensure that actual disbursements align
with the intended appropriations. Otherwise, a Supplemental Budget or
Augmentation should be prepared before disbursement if the true nature
of the transaction is not covered by the account definition in the Budget.

1.13. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
(a) Provide a detailed explanation for the existence of negative balances in
the payable accounts and determine accountable person for the
overpayment or adjust the financial statement if warranted;
(b) Ensure that all required information is provided in the schedules and that
current transactions involving permanent accounts in the financial
statements are adequately supported by schedules, including details
such as date of check, disbursement/journal entry voucher number,
payee, transaction description, amount, and other relevant information;
(c) Disclose in the Notes to FS all information required under IPSAS as well
as those included in the prescribed template; and
(d) Ensure that account names used are in accordance with the Revised
Chart of Accounts for LGUs.

We recommended and Management agreed to initiate action to cleanse its
books by following the procedures enumerated in Item 7.0 of the Circular in
the conduct of investigation in case of losses of documents evidencing the
financial transactions and/or records of accountabilities and Item 8.0 in the
derecognition of dormant accounts and make appropriate actions thereof.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
(a) Notify the heir/s of the late Municipal Treasurer of the existence of the
unpaid terminal leave benefits, the deductions to be made including the
unremitted collections of job orders under her supervision and the
unliquidated cash advances, the possibility of derecognition if left
unclaimed, and the documentary requirements to support the claim;
(b) Prepare the Quarterly Schedules of Dormant Accounts (SDA) to be
submitted on or before the 15th day of the ensuing month to the Audit
Team;
(c) Prepare schedules with complete details (i.e. date of
grant/receipts/disbursements, description, reference number)
duly supported by relevant documents; and conduct regular analysis and
validation of the existence of recorded accounts; and
(d) Secure and collate, for accounts below 10 years, all available documents
relative to said accounts such as ledgers, schedules, vouchers,
memorandum of agreement for fund transfers and all other documents
to establish validity and accuracy of the reported balances and make
appropriate action such as, but not limited to, issuance of demand letters,
salary deduction or filing of cases, when warranted; and
(e) Disclose comparative information in the Notes to Financial Statements
relative to the dormant accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed on the following:
(a) The Municipal Accountant derecognize the cost of repairs that fall below
the capitalization threshold and provide complete details of major
repairs to be included in the Supply Officer’s records;
(b) Before inventory-taking, the Supply Officer obtain a copy of the
subsidiary ledger from the Accounting Office and verify that all PPEs
recorded in the books are also reflected in their own records. After the
inventory count, a reconciliation should be conducted to identify PPEs
that are either missing from the location, unrecorded in the accounting
records, in poor condition, or due for disposal, and appropriate actions
should be taken; and
(c) The Inventory Committee conduct a comprehensive inventory count of
all PPEs, rather than limiting it to selected items. Moreover, verify the
existence and condition of the medical equipment omitted from the
RPCPPE and make appropriate recommendations.

We recommended and management agreed on the following:
(a) The Inventory Committee prepare the annual Report on the Physical
Count of Local Road Network of the LGU;
(b) The General Services Officer (or Municipal Treasurer) submit a year-end
Report of Local Road Network, maintain a Local Road Inventory and
Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for
all roads and their components;
(c) The Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and the
General Services Officer with the comprehensive description and cost
segregation of road components for road projects;
(d) The Municipal Accountant maintain a Local Road Network Ledger Card,
prepare working papers on the distribution of cost for the road
components, keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road
components for every road network, prepare lapsing schedule for the
computation of depreciation, and make appropriate disclosures in the
Notes to FS; and
(e) Concerned officials attend seminars on the Local Road Manual in
coordination with the DILG.

We recommended and management agreed on the following:
(a) The Municipal Engineer submit Inspection Reports of projects deemed
completed but not yet paid at year-end to be included in the
accomplishment of the municipality and as basis for setting-up of
Accounts Payable by the Municipal Accountant; and
(b) Management consider the following to increase rate of
accomplishment: (a) re-appropriate long-outstanding continuing
projects, (b) increase the Approve Budget for the Contract, and (c)
observe the guidelines on the Implementation of Early Procurement
Activities under GPPB Circular No. 06-2019.

(a) The LDRRMC update the Operational Guidelines for Stockpiling of Food
and Non-Food Items aligned with DSWD MC No. 24, S.2024, the LGA-
DILG Disaster Preparedness Manual for LGUs, and other relevant
issuances;
(b) In drafting the Bidding Documents for food items, the BAC-Technical
Working Group, in collaboration with the LDRRMO, explicitly define the
technical specifications. These should cover the type, weight, label and
marking requirements, packaging, shelf life, years of market presence as
well as other essential attributes such as color, appearance, taste, odor,
and other relevant descriptions. Furthermore, the BAC may refer to the
Bidding Documents for the “Purchase and delivery of pre-packed family
food packs or raw materials for the production of family food packs”
posted in the DSWD website under Procurement Opportunities as a
guide;
(c) Upon delivery of the goods, the person inspecting and accepting the
deliveries indicate the actual expiration date in the Inspection and
Acceptance Report and verify full compliance with the specified
technical requirements;
(d) The LGU prioritize the National Food Authority (NFA) as the primary
source for rice procurement, in accordance with Section 1 of Executive
Order No. 51. All subsequent procurement activities must continue to
prioritize NFA as a supplier unless a valid exemption is obtained and
properly documented. Additionally, the LGU may establish a Rice Supply
Agreement with the NFA to ensure availability of rice supply; and
(e) When procurement from sources other than the NFA is necessary, the
BAC should conduct and document a comprehensive cost-benefit
analysis and market survey to substantiate the decision. This
assessment should include price comparisons and quality evaluations
to ensure that the selected supplier offers the most favorable terms for
the government.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to constantly check
whether accountable forms are properly issued by making notations in the
Accountable Forms Logbook, demand in writing the immediate remittance or
deposit of collections held by AOs beyond the prescribed period, and install
further internal control measures or impose appropriate sanctions to ensure
compliance with the aforementioned rules.

We recommend and management agreed on the following actions:
(a) When planning procurement timelines, the BAC is encouraged to utilize the
GPPB Procurement Timelines Calculator for goods and infrastructure
projects, available in the GPPB website. This tool will help guarantee that
procurement is completed within the shortest possible period while adhering
to the maximum allowable timeframe;
(b) The Technical Working Group conducts a thorough evaluation and post
qualification process to prevent errors and inconsistencies, ascertaining
that winning bidders meet all necessary qualifications. Post-qualification
and contract documents must also be required in the Notice to the
Bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and Notice of award, respectively.
Additionally, the BAC must carefully review the TWG’s output before
affixing their signatures; and
(c) Before posting the Bidding Documents, the BAC review them to ensure
that all sections of the Bid Data Sheet are complete and accurate. This
process includes verifying the inclusion of a Safety Officer and First Aider
in the Organizational Chart, List of Key Personnel and Manpower
Utilization Schedule; specifying the minimum work experience required
for key personnel; listing the contract documents; and indicating the bid
security amount.

(a) The following reports be promptly submitted to oversight bodies: (a)
Annual Progress Report to the CCC within the first quarter of the
following year, (b) softcopies of LCCAP to the DILG and CCC within a
month from formulation or updating; and (c) the e-copy of the tagged AIP
and QAR to the CCC within a reasonable period;
(b) The approved climate change PAPs integrated in the AIP are aligned with
the LCCAP and prepared strictly in accordance with JMC No. 2015-01,
particularly on the accurate identification of the PAP whether as
adaptation or mitigation and the use of one (1) typology code for each
PAP; and
(c) In view of the issuance of the National Action Plan 2023-2050, the LCCAP
of the municipality be updated consistent with this Plan, when
warranted. The formulation or updating of the plan may be based on the
Enhanced LGU Guidebook on the Formulation of Local Climate Change
Action Plan Book 3 and Book 4, 2017 edition issued by the DILG and
Local Government Academy.

We recommended and Management agreed on the following:
(a) Management establish/reconstitute/strengthen the GFPS and the GAD M
& E Team and appropriate sufficient funding to effectively implement
GAD PPAs and ensure monitoring and assessment of GAD mechanisms;
(b) The Municipal Budget Officer verify that the identified GAD PPAs are
included in the Budget Message, LEP and BESF of the Municipal Budget
and that of the Barangay Budgets during review;
(c) The GAD Focal Person, in the submission of the AR, append the
essential attachments to facilitate review by the Provincial Government
and the Audit Team. Likewise, the GPB and the AR should be prepared
in accordance with the revised template prescribed in JMC No. 2016-01;
(d) The GFPS prepare the Annual Status Report on Institutional Mechanisms
for Gender Mainstreaming and submit the same to the DILG-RO; and
(e) The GAD M& E Team conduct the LGU GAD Evaluation Report for
submission to the DILG-RO.

We recommended and management agreed to enforce the requirement for obtaining
building permits; ensure the proper collection of building permit fees; establish a
Trust Fund account to allocate 15% of revenue for the Building Official’s office
operating expenses; and remit the 5% of the collected fees to the Bureau of
Treasury under the account of the DPWH Secretary.

We recommended and the MSWDO agreed to prepare the Agency Action Plan
and Accomplishment Report, ensuring that PPAS integrated into other
departments or special accounts are identified and incorporated accordingly.

We recommended and Management agreed on the following:
(a) Municipal officials and employees attend the training/seminar on the
RRPC and RRSA to enhance their understanding and compliance;
(b) The Municipal Treasurer deduct the full amount from the salaries and
other claims of liable individuals in cases where disallowances are
covered by a COA Order of Execution; and
(c) Responsible individuals immediately submit the required documents for
the settlement of suspensions. Otherwise, unresolved suspensions shall
be disallowed in audit.

Municipal Accountant

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Record adjusting journal entries to correct the balance of the affected accounts and conform with COA Circular No. 2015-009 on the appropriate account names to be used;
Ensure that permanent accounts in the financial statements are adequately supported by schedules with complete information such as date of check, disbursement/journal entry voucher number, payee, description of the transaction, amount and other relevant information; and
Reconcile the accounting and supply office records for inventory accounts and adhere with COA Circular No. 2022-004 on the maintenance of inventory records and other internal control system related to inventory. Likewise, strictly observe the supplemental guidelines under COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024 on the use of appropriate semi-expendable property accounts, maintenance of Semi-Expendable Property Card (SPCs) and Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card (SPLCs), issuance/replacement of ICS, use of the illustrative accounting entries and use of the Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards.

We recommended and Management agreed on the following:
The Municipal Accountant prepare the BRS on a timely basis, communicate errors with the bank for immediate adjustment and exclude the stale checks as outstanding and record them under the appropriate liability account;
The Municipal Treasurer sends written notices to payees a month before it becomes stale. In addition, list of unclaimed checks should be constantly posted in the social media account of the LGU; and
The Municipal Treasurer attach the cancelled checks in the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) for unclaimed checks and checks cancelled prior to preparation of RCI; and
The Municipal Accountant require as supporting document the original check in case of replacement of cancelled checks.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to:
Employ stricter internal controls and carefully monitor the proper issuance of accountable forms and the timely remittance and deposit of collections; and
Issue demand letter to present and former accountable officers and require them to submit documents as proof of remittances or deposits. Otherwise, deduct their balances from their salaries.

We recommended and the BAC agreed on the following:
Strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 particularly on the conduct of pre-procurement conference, eligibility checking based only on the minimum documentary requirements, submission of post-qualification and other contract documents, approval by the HoPE of the BAC recommending award, and other relevant provisions;
Post-qualification documents under Section 34.2 and other contract documents enumerated in Section 37.2.3 (f) may be included in the Checklist of Technical and Financial Documents but a different line item should be inserted, say, “Post-qualification and Other Contract Documents” since it has been a practice that these were submitted together with the Technical and Financial Proposals. However, they should not be included for purposes of determining the bidder with the lowest calculated bid. If not submitted during bid opening, the following may be done:
The BAC list the “post-qualification documents” in the Notice to Bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and require such bidder to submit them within five (5) days from receipt for post-qualification purposes. Otherwise, the bidder should be disqualified due to non-submission. Moreover, this notice should be signed by the BAC Chairman; and/or
The BAC enumerate the “other contract documents” in the Notice of Award to be submitted together with the Performance Security.
Update the Transmittal to the Audit Team to include all the necessary documentary requirements.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to use the prescribed Bidding Documents for goods and infrastructure projects, prepare the same in accordance with the standard forms and accurately and completely accomplish it.

We recommended and the BAC agreed on the following:
Strictly comply with the submission of the DOLE-approved Construction Safety and Health Program during contract signing as an integral part of the Contract Agreement; otherwise, the bidder shall be disqualified pursuant to Section 40.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184;
Include in the Bid Data Sheet (BDS) under the List of Key Personnel the required number and category of Safety Officer and Health Personnel to be provided by the contractor for the specific project at hand; and
Require bidders, through the Bidding Documents, to specify in the Organizational Chart the Safety Officer and the Occupational Health Personnel.
We also recommend and the Municipal Engineer agreed to:
Direct the Project Engineers to take photos of the personal protective equipment and the workers using them, the treatment room and medical kits, the Safety Officer and health personnel during their visits and the like to ensure compliance with RA No. 11058; and
Deduct from the Statement of Work Accomplished or billing of the contractor the amount allocated for CSHP in the POW without Safety Officer and Occupational Health Personnel or with Safety Officer but no DOLE-approved CSHP, and no submitted photos mentioned above; otherwise, the same shall be disallowed in audit and be liable thereon.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct all Department Heads to oblige the AO to secure from the Accountant a “Certification that previous cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the books” before approval of the Itinerary of Travel or Obligation Request and conform with all the rules and regulations relative to the grant, utilization and settlement of cash advances.
We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
demand liquidation of previous cash advances before granting additional advances even if the prescribed period of liquidation has not elapsed; and
deduct from the salaries, benefits and all other claims of the AOs their existing cash advances. Otherwise, a formal demand shall be made by the Audit Team and the filing of administrative cases or criminal case may ensue.

We recommended and Management agreed on the following:
Deduct in full the disallowed amount from the payrolls of persons liable, subject to the minimum net take home pay and other claims due them since there is no approved installment payment;
The Local Chief Executive direct responsible persons to immediately submit the required documents for transactions suspended in audit; otherwise, the same shall be disallowed in audit; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to make appropriate disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements respecting the reconciliation of the SASDC and subsidiary ledger in order to effectively monitor the balances of Notice of Suspension and Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:

draw a journal entry to correct the balance of the affected accounts and henceforth, check the proper account titles in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 before signing the journal entry voucher;

present the appropriate disclosures in the Notes to FS as required under COA Circular No. 2016-004 and other relevant disclosures to help readers in better understanding and appreciation of the financial statements; and

remit the taxes, premium contributions and loan repayments in full within the prescribed period and make the appropriate disclosures, adjustments or refunds at the end of the year, if warranted.


We also recommended and Management agreed that:

concerned department heads submit utilization report of inventories to the Municipal Accountant to prevent misstatements of the financial statements due to long outstanding inventories already consumed but not recorded in the books; and

The Local Chief Executive enjoin the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Engineer to comply with their duties and responsibilities under COA Circular No. 2015-008 on the accounting and reporting guidelines of local road asset management system.
observations.managementAction:

The delineation of projects under the jurisdiction of the Municipality is still ongoing.
observations.reason:
Some of the new employees failed to secure their numbers. On the other hand, there were delay in the updating of records and immediate reconciliation of the unremitted balances.

No physical count was made since they are still determining projects which are appropriately under the jurisdiction of the LGU.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:

draw a journal entry to correct the balance of the affected accounts and henceforth, check the proper account titles in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 before signing the journal entry voucher;
present the appropriate disclosures in the Notes to FS as required under COA Circular No. 2016-004 and other relevant disclosures to help readers in better understanding and appreciation of the financial statements; and
remit the taxes, premium contributions and loan repayments in full within the prescribed period and make the appropriate disclosures, adjustments or refunds at the end of the year, if warranted.
We also recommended and Management agreed that:
concerned department heads submit utilization report of inventories to the Municipal Accountant to prevent misstatements of the financial statements due to long outstanding inventories already consumed but not recorded in the books; and
The Local Chief Executive enjoin the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Engineer to comply with their duties and responsibilities under COA Circular No. 2015-008 on the accounting and reporting guidelines of local road asset management system.

We recommended and Management agreed on the following:
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Assessor to reconcile their records and the former to make the necessary adjustments to correct the balance of the Land account; and
The Municipal Treasurer as the General Services Officer to conduct inventory of lands possessed by the LGU and prioritize its registration with the Land Registration Authority in order to fully protect the interest of the Municipality.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to:
strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 04-2020 with regards to the invitation of observers, preparation of bidding documents and the procedures to be undertaken in the various procurement stages;
attend seminars, together with the BAC Secretariat and TWG Members, in order to apprise themselves of the latest guidelines on RA No. 9184 and various GPPB issuances; and
carefully evaluate documents prepared by the Technical Working Group and BAC Secretariat to ensure accuracy of information and compliance with relevant rules and regulations.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the supplier to post warranty security after acceptance of the delivered goods or items. The warranty security may either be in the form of retention money equivalent to at least one percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) of every progress payment, or special bank guarantee equivalent to at least one percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) of the contract price for the procurement of goods.
6.8. We also recommended and the BAC agreed to utilize the new Contract Agreement Form incorporating therein the new provision required under GPPB Circular No. 2020-04.
8.7. We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the Memorandum of Agreement and the pertinent guidelines for fund transfers.

9.9. We recommended and the Municipal Assessor agreed to submit the necessary documents enumerated in Item 2 above and provide justification for failure to comply with the provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA Memorandum No. 92-751.
9.10. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed, upon receipt of the RLSDDP, to derecognize in the books of the LGU the subject property and the related accumulated depreciation and impairment loss. Consequently, a receivable account should be set up to record the accountability of the accountable officer.

10.7. We recommended and Management agreed to make certain that collections and remittances are remitted and deposited intact, advise the Audit Team in case of relinquishment of office of accountable officers, and demand full settlement of the unremitted balances.

11.5. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
strictly enforce the liquidation of cash advances within the reglementary period and require the accountable officer to refund the unspent amount of their cash advances upon submission of liquidation documents;
withhold the salaries of accountable officers who failed to liquidated their cash advances before January 20 of the ensuing year; and
establish the date of grant of cash advances recorded under Other Receivables and request for write-off, if warranted, applying the specific guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005.
11.6. We also recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to:
demand full payment of the cash shortage of the collector concerned and/or withhold his salary as job order; and
initiate action to collect long outstanding unremitted collections and sufficiently explain the existence of such and why no efforts have been made to collect the same.

12.8. We recommended and Management agreed that:
the Municipal Accountant explain the incurrence of significant negative balances and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted;
the Municipal Treasurer refund the unexpended balances to the source agency; otherwise, request that the same be reverted to the General Fund, if the MOA does not provide the disposition of unutilized balances; and
the Municipal Engineer immediately implement projects funded by other government agencies in order to prevent reversal of the said fund to the source agency and to show capability in handling high impact projects as basis for subsequent fund transfers.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant agreed to reconcile their records in order to reflect the accurate balances of RPT receivables both in the RPTAR and the financial statements. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant set-up the deferred RPT/SET receivable at the beginning of the year.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to exert more effort in order to improve collection by submitting the Certified List of RPT Delinquencies to the Sangguniang Bayan for the latter to assist in the enforcement of remedies for collection and to post the notice of time of collection of taxes and RPT delinquencies in the Guru Press in order to widely disseminate the tax campaign of the municipality and also to optimize the monthly subscription fees being paid to the latter.

We recommended and Management agreed to employ controls such as the issuance of Certification by the Municipal Accountant on the take home pay available for loan repayments before they avail of additional loans from banks and the employee’s Association.
17.11. We recommended and Management agreed to enforce the settlement of all audit suspensions and disallowances in accordance with the foregoing guidelines.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant draw a journal entry to adjust the affected accounts and strictly follow the recognition criteria of assets, liabilities, revenues and expenses set out in IPSASs.

accountability.

applicable procurement procedures and comply with documentary
requirements.

compare balances of accountable officers as appearing in their cashbook vis-à-vis Schedule of Cash-Local Treasury as of each month end in order to detect errors, reconcile the differences and make timely adjustments.

reconcile with the DSWD relative to the payment of assistance to the three (3) beneficiaries under Item 5(a) despite their receipt of the same through their cash card in order for the DSWD to rectify their record, as necessary; and

We recommended that management strictly comply with GPPB Circular No. 01-20 relative to the procurements during the COVID-19 pandemic; and
We also recommended that management instruct those concerned that all forms or documents should be properly, completely and accurately accomplished before the signatories affix their signatures.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure documentary requirements are complete before processing the cash advance. Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer  require the payees’ signatures or authorization letters, as necessary, before making payment .

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, verify from available records the status of the projects to determine which are already completed and due for reversion.

We recommended that the Local School Board prepare the Annual School Board Budget as basis for the authority to disburse funds under the SEF. Likewise, budget for the period of pandemic should only reflect the allowable expenses under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2017 and No. 2 s. 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
submit a schedule of settlements made on the NDs issued indicating the name of payor, amount paid, the ND No. to which it will be applied, certified true copy of official receipts/disbursement vouchers/payrolls where payment was made, if available, and other pertinent information or documents to facilitate issuance of NSSDC; and
maintain complete records/schedules for NSs and NDs including their settlements and reconcile with the Statement of Suspensions, Disallowances and Charges (SASDC) issued by the Audit Team.

enforce the COA Order of Execution (COE) by requiring the withholding of salaries or other compensation due the person liable in satisfaction of the disallowance; and
ensure that suspensions are settled within the prescribed period.
